set4.translation: The wife is sleeping in the city.
One horse is sleeping near the gate.
notes: 'unus' = one / a single — accept 'one (lone) horse' / 'a single horse'

set4.latin: regina donum accipit, et vultus eius laetus est.
set4.translation: The queen receives a gift, and her face is happy.

set4.latin: tristis puella portam aperit, et patrem videt.
set4.translation: The sad girl opens the gate, and sees her father.

set4.latin: hic iuvenis audit verba regis, et statim effugit.
set4.translation: This young man hears the words of the king, and immediately escapes.
notes: 'regis' gen sg = of the king / the king's

set4.latin: ceteri milites cibum petunt, sed pauci mariti cibum habent.
set4.translation: The rest of the soldiers seek food, but few husbands have food.
notes: 'ceteri' = the rest / the other / the remaining — accept 'the other soldiers' / 'the remaining soldiers'
'peto' = seek / look for / ask for — here 'seek' / 'look for'
accept 'but few of the husbands have food'

set4.latin: multi equi ad portam adveniunt; regina eos iam videt.
set4.translation: Many horses arrive at the gate; the queen now sees them.

set4.latin: haec uxor iam dormit, sed hic maritus donum quaerit.
set4.translation: This wife is now sleeping, but this husband is searching for a gift.
notes: 'quaero' = search for / look for / ask — here 'search for' / 'look for'

set4.latin: quamquam uxor tristis est, maritus eam amat.
set4.translation: Although the wife is sad, the husband loves her.

set8.translation: The wife was sleeping in the city.
This husband will want food again.

set8.latin: unus equus prope portam audiebat, et statim advenit.
set8.translation: One horse was listening near the gate, and immediately arrived.
notes: 'audio' here used intransitively — 'was listening' / 'was hearing things'
'advenit' form ambiguous (present/perfect) — in narrative past context, perfect 'arrived' fits best

set8.latin: regina donum accipiet, et vultus eius laetus erit.
set8.translation: The queen will receive a gift, and her face will be happy.

set8.latin: tristis puella portam aperiebat, et tum patrem videbat.
set8.translation: The sad girl was opening the gate, and then was seeing her father.
notes: accept 'saw her father' / 'could see her father' / 'kept seeing her father'

set8.latin: haec puella verba regis audit, et mox effugiet.
set8.translation: This girl hears the words of the king, and will soon escape.

set8.latin: ceteri milites cibum petent, sed pauci mariti cibum iam habebunt.
set8.translation: The rest of the soldiers will seek food, but few husbands will already have food.
notes: 'ceteri' = the rest / the other / the remaining

set8.latin: multos annos rex in urbe habitabat.
set8.translation: For many years the king was living in the city.
notes: 'multos annos' accusative of duration — 'for many years'
accept 'many years the king lived' as word-order variant

set8.latin: quamquam uxor tristis erat, maritus mox adveniet et eam amabit.
set8.translation: Although the wife was sad, the husband will soon arrive and will love her.

set8.latin: cur hic filius dormiebat? quod pater eum in silvam iam ducebat.
set8.translation: Why was this son sleeping? Because the father was already leading him into the wood.

set12.latin: maritus cibum cupiverat, et reginam rogavit.
set12.translation: The husband had desired food, and asked the queen.

set12.latin: hic rex eos iam audiverat, et cum paucis militibus discessit.
set12.translation: This king had already heard them, and departed with a few soldiers.

set12.latin: unus equus ad portam advenit, et tristem reginam conspexit.
set12.translation: One horse arrived at the gate, and caught sight of the sad queen.

set12.latin: multos annos uxor sua sola in silva habitaverat.
set12.translation: For many years his wife had lived alone in the wood.
notes: 'multos annos' = for many years (acc of duration)
'sua' = his / her own — here (if subject is implied to be someone else's wife) accept 'his own wife' / 'his wife'

set12.latin: regina hoc donum tandem accepit, et laeta erat.
set12.translation: The queen finally received this gift, and was happy.

set12.latin: "cur," inquit maritus, "in urbe dormiebas?" uxor nihil dixit.
set12.translation: "Why," said the husband, "were you sleeping in the city?" The wife said nothing.
notes: 'dormiebas' 2sg imperfect — 'were you sleeping' / 'used you to sleep'
'nihil' = nothing — accept 'the wife said nothing' / 'the wife did not say anything'

set12.latin: pauci milites portam aperuerunt, et ceteri effugerunt.
set12.translation: A few soldiers opened the gate, and the rest escaped.

set12.latin: maritus suam uxorem amaverat, sed quamquam eam videbat, nihil dixit.
set12.translation: The husband had loved his own wife, but although he was seeing her, he said nothing.
notes: 'suam' = his own — accept 'his wife' / 'his own wife'

set12.latin: ubi hic maritus advenit, omnes laetos vultus viderunt.
set12.translation: When this husband arrived, everyone saw happy faces.

notes: note: could also mean 'the gate was open' (predicate adjective) — context-dependent; in an isolated sentence, accept BOTH: 'had been opened' and 'was open'

set14.translation: The girl, (having been) heard, immediately escaped.
notes: accept 'the girl who had been heard escaped immediately'

set14.latin: donum tandem apertum erat, et regina laeta erat.
set14.translation: The gift had finally been opened, and the queen was happy.
Everyone did not recognise the words that had been heard.
notes: better rendering: 'Nobody recognised the heard words' or 'Everyone failed to recognise the words (once) heard'
'audita' PPP agreeing with 'verba' (n.pl.acc) — accept 'the words that had been heard'
'cognosco' perfect = recognised / got to know / found out

set14.latin: ubi portae apertae erant, pauci milites effugerunt.
set14.translation: When the gates had been opened, a few soldiers escaped.
notes: 'ubi + pluperf' = when (once) — accept 'once the gates had been opened'

set14.latin: cibus tandem acceptus erat, et maritus laetus erat.
set14.translation: The food had finally been received, and the husband was happy.

set14.latin: haec uxor quaesita erat; tandem pater eam in silva vidit.
set14.translation: This wife had been searched for; finally the father saw her in the wood.
notes: 'quaesita erat' pluperfect passive of 'quaero' — 'had been searched for' / 'had been sought'

set14.latin: illa porta aperta erat a marito, sed uxor iam dormiebat.
set14.translation: That gate had been opened by the husband, but the wife was already sleeping.
notes: 'a marito' = by the husband (agent with a/ab+abl)

set14.latin: ille dux relictus erat, et tristis in urbe clamabat.
set14.translation: That leader had been left behind, and was shouting sadly in the city.
notes: 'relictus erat' pluperfect passive of 'relinquo' — 'had been left behind' / 'had been abandoned'
'tristis' here agrees with 'dux' — 'sad' / 'the sad leader was shouting' / 'the leader, sad, was shouting' / 'the leader was shouting, sadly'

set16.latin: quamquam haec uxor tristis erat, maritus eius tandem donum pulchrum accepit.
set16.translation: Although this wife was sad, her husband finally received a beautiful gift.

set16.latin: ceteri milites ad portam advenerunt, et cum paucis amicis eam aperuerunt.
set16.translation: The rest of the soldiers arrived at the gate, and with a few friends opened it.

set16.latin: ubi unus iuvenis verba regis audivit, statim effugere conatus est.
set16.translation: When one young man heard the words of the king, he immediately tried to escape.

set16.latin: tantus erat vultus eius dei; omnes igitur milites perterriti effugerunt.
set16.translation: So great was the face of that god; therefore all the terrified soldiers escaped.
notes: 'eius' = his / that one's — here 'of that god' / 'his'

set16.latin: "cur," inquit regina, "in silva multos annos dormiebas?" ille nihil dixit.
set16.translation: "Why," said the queen, "were you sleeping in the wood for many years?" He said nothing.
notes: 'multos annos' = for many years (acc of duration)
'dormiebas' 2sg imperfect — 'were you sleeping' / 'did you (use to) sleep'

set16.latin: hic equus prope portam advenit; uxor eum statim vidit.
set16.translation: This horse arrived near the gate; the wife immediately saw it.

set16.latin: quo modo ille maritus filiam quaesivit? per urbem festinavit, et portas tantas aperuit.
set16.translation: How did that husband search for his daughter? He hurried through the city, and opened such huge gates.
notes: 'tantas' = so great / so huge / such — here agreeing with 'portas' (f.pl.acc)

set16.latin: postquam uxor dormivit, maritus suus dona in porta posuit.
set16.translation: After the wife slept, her own husband placed gifts at the gate.
notes: 'in + abl' (porta) = on / at / in — here 'at the gate' / 'on the gate'

set16.latin: "cur," inquit pater, "hoc donum mihi iam accipis?" filius tandem dixit: "quod te amo."
set16.translation: "Why," said the father, "are you now accepting this gift for me?" The son finally said: "Because I love you."
notes: 'mihi' dative — 'to me' / 'for me' / 'from me' (dat of advantage/disadvantage; context here makes 'from me' also possible — GCSE typical: 'for me')
'quod' here = because (conjunction introducing direct speech reason)

set16.latin: illae feminae tristes cibum cupiebant, sed pauci milites eas tandem viderunt, et in urbem duxerunt.
set16.translation: Those sad women wanted food, but few soldiers finally saw them, and led them into the city.
